2606.04781.title: AIP: A Graph Representation for Learning and Governing Agent Skills
2606.04781.paper_summary: Agent Skills today consist largely of free-form prose requiring the agent to read, interpret, and re-derive how to act in every session. This imposes two compounding costs: reduced reliability on implementation-heavy tasks, and difficulty in skill creation and improvement, since editing prose is a fragile process that both humans and agents struggle with, particularly for domain-specific procedural knowledge underrepresented in model training. The Agent Instruction Protocol (AIP) addresses both by modeling a skill as a directed execution graph: discrete steps as nodes backed by deterministic scripts or natural-language descriptions, connected by explicit typed input/output edges, and governed by a schema-validated YAML specification. A compiler meta-skill translates existing human-written skills into this form. The benefits are twofold. First, compiling human-written skills to AIP raised Claude Sonnet's mean task reward from 0.60 to 0.71 and pass rate from 53% to 67% across 27 real agent tasks from SkillsBench - a statistically significant gain (Wilcoxon signed-rank p = 0.011), winning 12 tasks to 2 with 13 ties - often in less wall-clock time. The graph delivers vetted, runnable units to the agent rather than asking it to re-derive code, commands, and tool calls from natural language. Second, on creation and improvement, because each skill is schema-validated, functionally testable, and addressable node-by-node, failures can be diagnosed and repaired precisely. Two authored-skill failures were traced to the script level. After adjusting the AIP spec and recompiling, both recovered with zero regressions (one task going from 0/5 to 5/5), turning skill improvement into a measurable tuning loop rather than a prose rewrite. That same graph structure supports corpus-level governance and skill introspection, and provides a natural action space for reinforcement learning over skills.
2606.04781.paper_summary_zh: 代理技能目前主要由自由形式的散文組成，要求代理在每次會議中閱讀、解釋並重新推導如何行動。這帶來了兩個累積的成本：對於實施密集型任務的可靠性降低，以及技能創建和改進的困難，因為編輯散文是一個脆弱的過程，人類和代理都難以應對，特別是對於在模型訓練中表現不足的特定領域程序知識。代理指令協議（AIP）通過將技能建模為有向執行圖來解決這兩個問題：離散步驟作為由確定性腳本或自然語言描述支持的節點，通過明確的類型化輸入/輸出邊連接，並由模式驗證的YAML規範管理。一個編譯器元技能將現有的人類編寫的技能轉換為這種形式。其好處有兩方面。首先，將人類編寫的技能編譯為AIP使Claude Sonnet的平均任務獎勵從0.60提高到0.71，通過27個來自SkillsBench的真實代理任務的通過率從53%提高到67%——這是一個統計上顯著的增益（Wilcoxon符號秩p = 0.011），贏得12個任務，輸掉2個，平局13次——通常用時更少。該圖向代理提供經過審核的可運行單元，而不是要求其從自然語言重新推導代碼、命令和工具調用。其次，在創建和改進方面，由於每個技能都是模式驗證的、功能可測試的，並且可以逐節點尋址，故障可以精確診斷和修復。兩個編寫的技能故障被追溯到腳本層級。在調整AIP規範並重新編譯後，兩者均以零回歸恢復（一個任務從0/5變為5/5），將技能改進轉變為可測量的調整循環，而不是散文重寫。相同的圖結構支持語料庫級治理和技能內省，並為技能的強化學習提供了一個自然的行動空間。
2606.04781.author: Zachary Blumenfeld et.al.
